question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: append()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that maxBlockItems has a minimum of 1000 (@Min(1000)) to observe that a deployment configured at maxBlockItems=1000 is far more susceptible to block-overflow attacks, since submitting just 1001 transactions in a single block triggers BlockStreamException and onError(), making it trivially easy to freeze block nodes with minimal transaction volume?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: streamBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send a valid SubscribeStreamResponse with BLOCK_ITEMS but with an empty blockItems list inside the BlockItemSet, causing onBlockItemSet() to log a warning and return without processing, so the block assembly never completes, the timeout() counter keeps running, and eventually grpcCall.read() times out, causing the streaming loop to exit without a completed block?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: onError()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of exponential backoff in readmitDelay (it is a fixed duration, not increasing) to mount a sustained attack where they cause maxSubscribeAttempts failures every readmitDelay period, keeping all block nodes perpetually cycling between active and inactive states, causing a block ingestion freeze of readmitDelay duration every cycle?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getNode()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential node iteration in BlockNodeSubscriber.getNode() (iterating nodeList in order) to cause a worst-case scenario where the attacker deactivates nodes in reverse priority order, forcing the subscriber to iterate through all inactive nodes before finding none with the block, maximizing the time spent in getNode() and delaying block ingestion?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: streamBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that errors.set(0) is called inside the streaming loop on every successful response (line: errors.set(0)), meaning that if the attacker sends maxSubscribeAttempts-1 successful responses followed by one error, the error counter never reaches the threshold, but the attacker can still cause significant per-block delays by making each block take much longer than average to stream?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: onError()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that when a block node is marked inactive, the readmitTime is set based on Instant.now() at the time of deactivation, and since there is no synchronization between multiple concurrent error triggers, a race condition could cause readmitTime to be set to a later time than intended, extending the freeze beyond the configured readmitDelay?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getBlockRange()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getBlockRange() makes a synchronous blocking gRPC call with responseTimeout (400ms) deadline, and if the attacker can cause the block node's status service to respond slowly (e.g., via resource exhaustion), every call to getNode() will block for up to 400ms per node, causing cumulative delays that push block ingestion time well beyond 500% of average block time?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: onError()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the interaction between maxSubscribeAttempts=3 and a single-node deployment to cause a complete block ingestion freeze with just 3 consecutive streaming failures, since there is no fallback node, and the single node is inactive for readmitDelay (60 seconds), which is 2000-3000% of the average 2-3 second block time?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: streamBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send a gRPC stream that delivers block items very slowly (one item per responseTimeout interval), causing the BlockAssembler.timeout() to approach zero before the block is complete, and when timeout() returns a near-zero value, grpcCall.read() times out immediately, causing the block to be abandoned and onError() to be triggered?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: onError()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the errors counter is an AtomicInteger that is only reset to 0 after reaching maxSubscribeAttempts (not on successful streaming), meaning that partial error accumulation persists across successful blocks, so an attacker who causes maxSubscribeAttempts-1 errors followed by one success, then one more error, can deactivate the node with fewer total errors than expected?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: append()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that pendingCount accumulates across multiple BlockItemSet messages within the same block (not reset between sets), so that by sending many small BlockItemSet messages each containing a few items, the attacker can gradually push pendingCount above maxBlockItems without triggering any per-message size limit, causing BlockStreamException only when the total exceeds the threshold?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: getBlockRange()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getBlockRange() is called for every node on every getNode() invocation (not cached), so that in a deployment with many block nodes, the attacker can cause all status checks to time out simultaneously, making getNode() spend N * responseTimeout (N * 400ms) before throwing BlockStreamException, causing a cumulative delay proportional to the number of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: onError()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that when active is set to false in onError(), the tryReadmit(false) check in getNode() will skip the node, but the fallback loop that force-readmits inactive nodes only does so if hasBlock() returns true, meaning that if the attacker also causes getBlockRange() to return EMPTY_BLOCK_RANGE for inactive nodes, the force-readmit path is never triggered and the freeze persists for the full readmitDelay?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: streamBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the gRPC channel's maxInboundMessageSize is set to maxStreamResponseSize.toBytes() (36MB cast to int), and if maxStreamResponseSize is configured to a value exceeding Integer.MAX_VALUE bytes, the cast to int overflows to a negative value, causing the gRPC channel to reject all messages and triggering onError() on every streaming attempt?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/block/StreamProperties.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: onError()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that readmitDelay has no upper bound constraint (only @DurationMin(seconds=10)), and if an operator misconfigures readmitDelay to a very large value (e.g., Duration.ofDays(1)) due to a typo or unit confusion, a single attack causing maxSubscribeAttempts failures would freeze block ingestion for 24 hours, which is astronomically beyond 500% of average block time?
